course_description:
This course introduces students to cost accounting concepts relevant to planning and controlling and decision making activities within organisations. Focusing on the accounting systems that managers use in their decisions about resource allocation and performance evaluation, especially within the manufacturing environment.

Management accounting information is used to support planning, control, and decision making in organisations. The course is structured around three integrated themes:

course_aims: This course aims for students to understand cost behaviour , relevant costing, capital expenditures, cost allocation, cost management, budgetary control and environmental management accounting . The course also aims to equip students with group work skills and skills in concisely communicating management accounting information.

learning_outcomes.description: Recognise and explain the role of management accounting in the planning, control and decision making activities of organisations.
Understand variable and fixed costs, and apply that knowledge to cost-volume-profit analysis.
Understand and apply relevant costing techniques.
Understand capital expenditure analysis and work in a group to apply that understanding to prepare and concisely communicate a 'real-world' business case.
Understand and apply traditional and activity-based overhead costing methods, and explain the circumstances in which a particular method is appropriate.
Understand budgeting systems, prepare budgets and flexible budget variance analyses and explain the rationale for using these, and their behavioural effects.
Develop an awareness of strategy, balanced scorecards and sustainability management accounting.

assessment_details.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The In-Semester Examination will be held on a Saturday after the content of Lecture 4 and its corresponding tutorial have been delivered. There are three possible dates; please refer to the UQ Calendar for details. This exam is an on-campus, paper-based, closed book examination invigilated by UQ-appointed invigilators. It will assess material from Lectures and Tutorials (Topics) 1–4 and will comprise multiple-choice, short-answer, and practical/problem-solving questions. Multiple-choice questions will be computer-marked, while short-answer and practical/problem-solving questions will be marked based on both workings and final answers. You must bring your student ID to the examination. Results will be released via the course Blackboard site. Further details regarding the examination format will be provided on Blackboard. Approved deferred In-Semester Examinations will also be held on a Saturday; please refer to the UQ Calendar for the relevant date. AI Statement: This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Group Case Study
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: You are required to work in a group of three or four to submit the case study assignment. Groups of three and groups of four are assessed using the same marking criteria, with identical expectations for the case submission. Developing effective group work skills and the ability to communicate analysis concisely in a business style are important capabilities for graduates and managers. Students can form a group with anyone enrolled in this course. Students must arrange themselves into groups. If you need assistance joining or forming a group, please contact the Course Tutors in the first instance. A peer assessment task must also be completed for this group assignment. The results of the peer assessment may impact your final result for this assessment. Case studies are designed to enhance learning by requiring the application of course material to a real or realistic business organisation. The case study is due by 11:00 am (Brisbane time) on Friday, 1 May 2026 . Further information will be made available on Blackboard when the case is released. AI Statement: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
See Blackboard for the submission requirements - these must be strictly applied in order to comply with the University policies and procedures for student equity.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
